passages.passage_text: In an extensive study of reading habits of magazine readers, it was found that an average of forty-five people actually read each copy of the most popular weekly news magazine. Forty-five copies of any favourite magazine that are sold each month are actually read by 48,000 to 60,000 people. The estimate assumes that A) individual magazine readers generally enjoy more than one type of magazine B) some readers of Poets and Poetry subscribe to magazines C) the ratio of readers to copies is the same for Poets and Poetry as for other news magazines D) the number of readers of the weekly news magazine is similar to the number of readers of Poet and Poetry. B) most of the readers enjoy sharing copies of their favourite magazines with friends and family members.

questions.subject: verbal
questions.body: Which assumption is made in the argument?

choices.a: individual magazine readers generally enjoy more than one type of magazine
choices.b: some readers of Poets and Poetry subscribe to magazines
choices.c: the ratio of readers to copies is the same for Poets and Poetry as for other news magazines
choices.d: the number of readers of the weekly news magazine is similar to the number of readers of Poet and Poetry

questions.subject: verbal
questions.body: It is a truism of military science that "Generals always prepare for the war of the past when public officials generally spend their efforts on problems that were resolved - one way or another - years before. By the time a public issue reaches the consciousness of enough of the citizenry to become a high priority of our elected leaders, the problem is usually so changed that government efforts can significantly affect it. All of the following are assumed in the argument above EXCEPT:

choices.a: Most of the public problems tend to evolve towards a point at which the government can do little to control them
choices.b: Political and military leaders are both prone to react tardily to change in their fields
choices.c: Issues attain importance for public officials when large number of citizens are concerned about them
choices.d: Planning policies, solely based on past experiences are likely to be ineffective
choices.e: The government officials can generally do little to influence the course of public policy

questions.subject: verbal
questions.body: Questions (19-20): Government spending in support of pure research is often treated as somehow wasteful, as though only immediate technological application can justify government endeavour. Yet, unless the well of basic knowledge is continually replenished through pure research, the flow of technological applications will dry up. Today's pure research may appear to be irrelevant, but no one can tell what good it may yield someday. One might as well ask: "Of what use is a newborn baby?" 1. There is a time lag between the discovery of a new phenomenon and the discovery of a practical application for it. Pure research is more costly and time-consuming than the attempt to develop new technologies.

questions.subject: verbal
questions.body: Which of the following would the author compare a "newborn baby" mentioned in the last sentence of the passage?

choices.a: A technological breakthrough based on pure research
choices.b: Government funding in support of scientific endeavour
choices.c: Public support for spending on pure research
choices.d: The flow of public benefits from new technological developments
choices.e: A scientific discovery with no immediate apparent application

questions.subject: verbal
questions.body: One major obligation of the social psychologist is to provide his own discipline, the other social sciences, and interested laymen with conceptual tools that will increase the range and the reliability of their understanding of social phenomena. Beyond that, responsible government officials are today turning more frequently to social scientists for insights into the nature and solution of problems with which they are confronted. The above argument assumes that A) Social psychologists must have a strong background in other sciences as well as their own. B) A study of social psychology should be a part of the curriculum of government officials. C) The social scientist has an obligation to provide the means by which social phenomena may be understood by others. D) Social phenomena are little understood by those outside the field of social psychology. E) A good social psychologist is obligated principally by the need to solve interdisciplinary problems.

choices.a: Social psychologists must have a strong background in other sciences as well as their own
choices.b: A study of social psychology should be a part of the curriculum of government officials
choices.c: The social scientist has an obligation to provide the means by which social phenomena may be understood by others
choices.d: Social phenomena are little understood by those outside the field of social psychology
choices.e: A good social psychologist is obligated principally by the need to solve interdisciplinary problems

questions.subject: verbal
questions.body: New problems require new solutions. And new problems arise with new populations and new technologies. The solutions of these problems require new institutions and well as new political, economic, and social mechanisms. Yet, institutions and political and economic arrangements grow slowly and die slowly. Because old institutions die slowly, new institutions should be given every chance of success. The writer of the above passage makes which of the following assumptions?

choices.a: New institutions are needed because old institutions are inefficient
choices.b: New institutions are created in order to solve existing problems
choices.c: As old institutions are phased out, new ones take their place
choices.d: If there were no growth, old institutions would die more slowly
choices.e: Socio-technological change requires new forms of institutional arrangements
